question_text: What are the two types of React components?
explanation: React components can be written as plain functions (functional) or ES6 classes (class components).

question_text: What is the naming rule for React components?
option_b: Must start with an uppercase letter
correct_answer: Must start with an uppercase letter
explanation: React treats lowercase tags as DOM elements. Components must start with uppercase to be recognized by React.

question_text: What are props in React?
option_b: Read-only inputs passed from parent to child components
correct_answer: Read-only inputs passed from parent to child components
explanation: Props (properties) are how parent components pass data and functions down to child components.

question_text: Can a component modify its own props?
explanation: Props are immutable. A component must never modify its own props. Use state for mutable data instead.

question_text: What is the children prop in React?
option_b: The content nested between a component opening and closing tags
correct_answer: The content nested between a component opening and closing tags
explanation: props.children automatically contains whatever JSX or content is placed between the component tags.

question_text: What are defaultProps used for?
option_b: Providing fallback values when props are not supplied by the parent
correct_answer: Providing fallback values when props are not supplied by the parent
explanation: defaultProps defines default values for props ensuring a component works even when props are omitted.

question_text: What is the purpose of PropTypes in React?
option_b: Runtime type-checking to warn when incorrect prop types are passed
correct_answer: Runtime type-checking to warn when incorrect prop types are passed
explanation: PropTypes validates prop types in development mode logging warnings when wrong types are received.

question_text: How do you export a React component as a default export?
explanation: export default makes the component the primary export of the file importable without curly braces.

question_text: How do you import a default exported component?
explanation: Default exports are imported without curly braces. The name you import can be anything you choose.

question_text: What is a named export in React?
option_b: An export using curly braces on import like import { Button }
correct_answer: An export using curly braces on import like import { Button }
explanation: Named exports use curly braces when importing. A file can have many named exports but only one default.

question_text: What is component composition in React?
option_b: Building complex UIs by combining smaller reusable components
correct_answer: Building complex UIs by combining smaller reusable components
explanation: Composition is the preferred React pattern for reuse. Components wrap other components to build complex UIs.

question_text: What is prop drilling?
option_b: Passing props through many layers of components that do not need them
correct_answer: Passing props through many layers of components that do not need them
explanation: Prop drilling occurs when data must pass through multiple intermediate components that do not use it.

question_text: What happens when you pass a function as a prop?
option_b: The child component receives the function and can call it to communicate upward
option_c: It is converted to a string
correct_answer: The child component receives the function and can call it to communicate upward
explanation: Passing callbacks as props allows children to notify parents of events like button clicks.

question_text: What is a pure component in React?
option_b: A component that always returns the same output for the same input props
correct_answer: A component that always returns the same output for the same input props
explanation: Pure components have no side effects and return consistent output making them predictable and testable.

question_text: What is the difference between a functional and class component?
option_b: Functional is a JS function; class extends React.Component and has lifecycle methods
correct_answer: Functional is a JS function; class extends React.Component and has lifecycle methods
explanation: Functional components are simpler. With hooks they can do everything class components can.

question_text: What is the purpose of the key prop on a component in a list?
option_b: Helping React uniquely identify and efficiently update list items
correct_answer: Helping React uniquely identify and efficiently update list items
explanation: Without keys React re-renders the entire list. Keys allow React to update only changed items.

question_text: Can you use conditional rendering with component props?
option_b: Yes you can pass different components or values based on a condition
correct_answer: Yes you can pass different components or values based on a condition
explanation: Props can be any JS expression including conditionals: <Comp icon={isActive ? <Sun/> : <Moon/>} />.

question_text: What is re-rendering in React?
option_a: Deleting a component from the DOM
option_b: React recalculating and updating the UI when state or props change
correct_answer: React recalculating and updating the UI when state or props change
explanation: When state or props change React re-renders the affected component and its children.

question_text: What is the Virtual DOM in React?
option_b: A lightweight in-memory representation of the real DOM used for diffing
correct_answer: A lightweight in-memory representation of the real DOM used for diffing
explanation: React maintains a virtual DOM and diffs it with the previous version to apply minimal real DOM updates.

question_text: What is React reconciliation?
option_b: The process of comparing old and new virtual DOM to compute minimal DOM updates
correct_answer: The process of comparing old and new virtual DOM to compute minimal DOM updates
explanation: Reconciliation is Reacts algorithm for determining what changed and applying the smallest set of DOM changes.

question_text: What does the componentDidMount lifecycle method do?
option_b: Runs code after the component is first added to the DOM
correct_answer: Runs code after the component is first added to the DOM
explanation: componentDidMount fires once after the initial render. It is the right place to fetch data or set up subscriptions.

question_text: What does componentWillUnmount do?
option_b: Runs cleanup code before the component is removed from the DOM
correct_answer: Runs cleanup code before the component is removed from the DOM
explanation: componentWillUnmount is called just before a component is destroyed. Use it to clear timers and subscriptions.

question_text: What does the render method do in a class component?
option_b: Returns the JSX that describes the component UI
correct_answer: Returns the JSX that describes the component UI
explanation: The render method is required in class components. It must be a pure function returning JSX.

question_text: What is a React portal?
option_b: A way to render children into a DOM node outside the parent component hierarchy
correct_answer: A way to render children into a DOM node outside the parent component hierarchy
explanation: ReactDOM.createPortal(child, container) renders content outside the current DOM tree e.g. modals.

question_text: What is the purpose of React.StrictMode?
option_b: A development tool that detects potential problems and deprecated APIs
correct_answer: A development tool that detects potential problems and deprecated APIs
explanation: StrictMode intentionally double-invokes functions in development to surface side effects and warnings.

question_text: What is a React hook?
option_b: A special function that lets functional components use state and lifecycle features
correct_answer: A special function that lets functional components use state and lifecycle features
explanation: Hooks were introduced in React 16.8 allowing functional components to use state effects and more.

question_text: Which hook is used to add state to a functional component?
explanation: useState is the primary hook for adding local mutable state to a functional component.

question_text: What does useState return?
option_b: An array with the state value and an updater function
correct_answer: An array with the state value and an updater function
explanation: useState returns [currentValue, setterFunction]. Destructure it: const [count, setCount] = useState(0).

question_text: What is the argument passed to useState?
option_b: The initial value of the state variable
correct_answer: The initial value of the state variable
explanation: useState(initialValue) sets the starting value. It can be any type: number string boolean object or array.

question_text: What happens when you call the useState setter function?
option_b: React schedules a re-render with the new state value
correct_answer: React schedules a re-render with the new state value
explanation: Calling the setter triggers a re-render and React updates the component with the new state.

question_text: Can you use multiple useState hooks in one component?
explanation: React allows multiple useState calls. Each hook manages its own independent state variable.

question_text: What is the rule about calling hooks inside conditions or loops?
option_a: You can freely use them anywhere
option_b: You must not call hooks inside conditions loops or nested functions
option_c: Only useState can be called conditionally
option_d: Only class hooks can be conditional
correct_answer: You must not call hooks inside conditions loops or nested functions
explanation: Hooks must always be called at the top level of a component so React can track their call order correctly.

question_text: Which hook is used to perform side effects in a functional component?
explanation: useEffect lets you run side effects like data fetching subscriptions and DOM manipulation after render.

question_text: When does useEffect run by default with no dependency array?
explanation: Without a dependency array useEffect runs after every single render of the component.

question_text: What does an empty dependency array [] in useEffect mean?
option_b: Run only once after the initial mount
correct_answer: Run only once after the initial mount
explanation: An empty array [] tells React the effect has no dependencies so it only runs once after the first render.

question_text: What is the dependency array in useEffect?
option_b: A list of values that control when the effect re-runs
correct_answer: A list of values that control when the effect re-runs
explanation: The effect re-runs whenever any value in the dependency array changes between renders.

question_text: What is the cleanup function in useEffect?
option_b: A function returned from useEffect that runs before the next effect or on unmount
correct_answer: A function returned from useEffect that runs before the next effect or on unmount
explanation: Return a function from useEffect to clean up: clear timers cancel subscriptions or abort requests.

question_text: How do you fetch data inside useEffect?
option_b: Using fetch or axios inside useEffect and setting state with the response
correct_answer: Using fetch or axios inside useEffect and setting state with the response
explanation: Place the async fetch call inside useEffect. Use an async IIFE or a named async function inside the effect.

question_text: What is the useRef hook used for?
option_b: Accessing a DOM element directly or storing a mutable value without causing re-renders
correct_answer: Accessing a DOM element directly or storing a mutable value without causing re-renders
explanation: useRef returns a ref object whose .current property persists across renders without causing re-renders.

question_text: How do you attach a ref to a DOM element?
option_b: By passing the ref object to the ref prop of a JSX element
correct_answer: By passing the ref object to the ref prop of a JSX element
explanation: const inputRef = useRef(); then <input ref={inputRef} />. Access the DOM node via inputRef.current.

question_text: What is the difference between useRef and useState?
option_b: useRef changes do not trigger re-renders while useState changes do
correct_answer: useRef changes do not trigger re-renders while useState changes do
explanation: useRef is for persisting values or DOM access without re-rendering. useState is for reactive data.

question_text: What does useContext do?
option_b: Reads the value of a React Context avoiding prop drilling
correct_answer: Reads the value of a React Context avoiding prop drilling
explanation: useContext(MyContext) returns the current context value provided by the nearest matching Provider above.

question_text: What is useMemo used for?
option_b: Caching the result of an expensive calculation so it is not recomputed every render
correct_answer: Caching the result of an expensive calculation so it is not recomputed every render
explanation: useMemo(fn, deps) memoizes a computed value and only recomputes when the listed dependencies change.

question_text: What is useCallback used for?
option_b: Memoizing a function so the same reference is returned across renders
correct_answer: Memoizing a function so the same reference is returned across renders
explanation: useCallback(fn, deps) returns a stable function reference preventing child re-renders from function prop changes.

question_text: What is useReducer used for?
option_b: Managing complex state logic using a reducer function and dispatch
correct_answer: Managing complex state logic using a reducer function and dispatch
explanation: useReducer is an alternative to useState for complex state with multiple sub-values or transition logic.

question_text: How do you create a custom hook?
option_b: Writing a function starting with use that can call other hooks
correct_answer: Writing a function starting with use that can call other hooks
explanation: Custom hooks are plain JS functions prefixed with use. They encapsulate and reuse stateful logic.

question_text: What rule must a custom hook follow regarding its name?
option_b: Must start with the word use
correct_answer: Must start with the word use
explanation: The use prefix tells React and linting tools that the function follows hook rules.

question_text: Can hooks be called inside regular JavaScript functions outside a component?
option_b: No only inside React functional components or other custom hooks
correct_answer: No only inside React functional components or other custom hooks
explanation: Hooks must be called inside React functional components or custom hooks. This is a strict rule.

question_text: What is the eslint-plugin-react-hooks used for?
option_b: Enforcing the rules of hooks automatically in your code editor
correct_answer: Enforcing the rules of hooks automatically in your code editor
explanation: The ESLint plugin warns you when you break hook rules like conditional calls or wrong naming.

question_text: What happens if you forget a dependency in the useEffect dependency array?
option_b: The effect may run with stale values causing bugs
correct_answer: The effect may run with stale values causing bugs
explanation: Missing dependencies cause the effect to capture old values in a closure leading to stale data bugs.

question_text: What is the useState lazy initialization pattern?
option_b: Passing a function to useState so the initial value is computed only once
option_c: Using useEffect to set initial state
correct_answer: Passing a function to useState so the initial value is computed only once
explanation: Passing a function useState(() => compute()) avoids running expensive initialization on every render.

question_text: Can you update state based on the previous state value in useState?
option_b: Yes by passing a function to the setter: setCount(prev => prev + 1)
correct_answer: Yes by passing a function to the setter: setCount(prev => prev + 1)
explanation: Using a callback in the setter guarantees you get the latest state value avoiding stale closure bugs.

question_text: What is the useLayoutEffect hook?
option_b: Fires synchronously after DOM mutations but before the browser paints
correct_answer: Fires synchronously after DOM mutations but before the browser paints
explanation: useLayoutEffect is like useEffect but fires synchronously useful for measuring DOM nodes before paint.

question_text: How do you abort a fetch request when a component unmounts using useEffect?
option_b: Return a cleanup function that calls abortController.abort()
correct_answer: Return a cleanup function that calls abortController.abort()
explanation: Create an AbortController pass its signal to fetch and call abort() in the cleanup to prevent memory leaks.

question_text: What does the useId hook generate?
option_b: A unique stable ID consistent between server and client renders
correct_answer: A unique stable ID consistent between server and client renders
explanation: useId introduced in React 18 generates unique IDs for accessibility attributes like aria-labelledby.

question_text: What is the main difference between state and a regular variable in a component?
option_b: State persists across re-renders and triggers re-render when changed unlike regular variables
option_d: State is only for class components
correct_answer: State persists across re-renders and triggers re-render when changed unlike regular variables
explanation: Regular variables reset on every render. State is preserved by React between renders.

question_text: What is the purpose of the second argument in useEffect?
option_b: Controls when the effect runs based on dependency changes
correct_answer: Controls when the effect runs based on dependency changes
explanation: The dependency array as the second argument tells React when to re-run the effect.

question_text: How do you prevent a useEffect from running on every render?
option_b: Add a dependency array with relevant values
correct_answer: Add a dependency array with relevant values
explanation: Providing a dependency array restricts the effect to only re-run when listed dependencies change.

question_text: What is React state batching?
option_b: Grouping multiple state updates into a single re-render for performance
correct_answer: Grouping multiple state updates into a single re-render for performance
explanation: React batches multiple setState calls in event handlers into one re-render. React 18 extended this everywhere.

question_text: Can you call hooks inside a class component?
option_b: No hooks only work in functional components
correct_answer: No hooks only work in functional components
explanation: Hooks are exclusively for functional components. Class components use this.state and lifecycle methods instead.

question_text: What is the purpose of the useDebugValue hook?
option_b: Displaying a custom label for a custom hook in React DevTools
correct_answer: Displaying a custom label for a custom hook in React DevTools
explanation: useDebugValue(value) shows a label next to your custom hook in the React DevTools inspector.

question_text: How do you share logic between multiple components using hooks?
option_b: Extracting the logic into a custom hook used by both components
correct_answer: Extracting the logic into a custom hook used by both components
explanation: Custom hooks are the idiomatic React way to share stateful logic without duplicating code.

question_text: What does it mean that hooks preserve state between renders?
option_b: React remembers the state value and keeps it until the component unmounts
correct_answer: React remembers the state value and keeps it until the component unmounts
explanation: React associates hook state with the component instance and preserves it across re-renders.

question_text: Can multiple components use the same custom hook?
option_a: No a hook belongs to one component
option_b: Yes each component gets its own independent copy of the hook state
option_c: Yes and they share the same state
correct_answer: Yes each component gets its own independent copy of the hook state
explanation: Custom hooks share logic not state. Each component using the hook gets its own isolated state.

question_text: What is the purpose of useState when managing a form input?
option_b: Storing the current input value so the input is controlled by React
correct_answer: Storing the current input value so the input is controlled by React
explanation: Storing input value in state and updating it with onChange makes the input a controlled component.

question_text: What is React Router?
option_b: A library for adding client-side navigation to React apps without page reloads
correct_answer: A library for adding client-side navigation to React apps without page reloads
explanation: React Router enables navigation between views in single-page apps keeping the URL in sync with the UI.

question_text: What does the BrowserRouter component do?
option_b: Provides routing context using the HTML5 history API for the app
correct_answer: Provides routing context using the HTML5 history API for the app
explanation: BrowserRouter wraps your app and uses the browser history API to enable clean URL-based routing.

question_text: What does the Route component render?
option_b: A component when the current URL matches its path prop
correct_answer: A component when the current URL matches its path prop
explanation: Route renders its element prop when the URL path matches providing declarative route definitions.

question_text: What is the Link component used for in React Router?
option_b: Client-side navigation without triggering a full page reload
correct_answer: Client-side navigation without triggering a full page reload
explanation: Link renders an anchor tag and uses the history API to update the URL without a full browser reload.

question_text: What is the NavLink component in React Router?
option_b: A Link that automatically adds an active class when the route matches
correct_answer: A Link that automatically adds an active class when the route matches
explanation: NavLink adds an active class or style to indicate the currently active route great for navigation menus.

question_text: What is a dynamic route in React Router?
option_b: A route using a URL parameter like /user/:id
correct_answer: A route using a URL parameter like /user/:id
explanation: Dynamic segments defined with a colon capture variable URL parts accessible via useParams.

question_text: What hook reads URL parameters from a dynamic route?
explanation: useParams returns an object of key-value pairs from dynamic route segments like {id} from /user/:id.

question_text: What hook provides the current URL location object?
explanation: useLocation returns the location object containing pathname search hash and state.

question_text: What hook allows programmatic navigation in React Router v6?
explanation: useNavigate returns a navigate function: navigate(/about) redirects the user programmatically.

question_text: What is the Outlet component in React Router v6?
option_b: A placeholder where nested child route components are rendered
correct_answer: A placeholder where nested child route components are rendered
explanation: Outlet is placed inside a parent route layout and renders whichever child route currently matches.

question_text: What is the Routes component in React Router v6?
option_b: A wrapper that renders only the first Route that matches the URL
correct_answer: A wrapper that renders only the first Route that matches the URL
explanation: Routes replaced Switch in v6. It renders exclusively the first matching Route eliminating order issues.

question_text: How do you create a 404 not found route in React Router?
option_b: Adding a Route with path=* at the end of Routes
correct_answer: Adding a Route with path=* at the end of Routes
explanation: A Route with path=* acts as a wildcard catching all unmatched URLs and rendering the 404 component.

question_text: What is the Navigate component in React Router v6?
option_b: A component that redirects to another URL when rendered
correct_answer: A component that redirects to another URL when rendered
explanation: Navigate renders a redirect. Useful for protecting routes: if not auth render Navigate to=/login.

question_text: What is a nested route in React Router?
option_b: A route defined inside another route sharing a parent layout
correct_answer: A route defined inside another route sharing a parent layout
explanation: Nested routes share a parent layout and render child content in the Outlet placeholder.

question_text: What is client-side routing?
option_b: Navigation handled entirely in the browser without full page reloads
correct_answer: Navigation handled entirely in the browser without full page reloads
explanation: Client-side routing updates the URL and renders new components in the browser without a server round trip.

question_text: What is state management in React?
option_b: Controlling and sharing data across components in a predictable way
correct_answer: Controlling and sharing data across components in a predictable way
explanation: State management ensures that data flows correctly across components and the UI stays in sync with data.

question_text: What is local state in React?
option_b: State managed within a single component using useState
correct_answer: State managed within a single component using useState
explanation: Local state lives inside a component managed by useState and is not directly accessible to other components.

question_text: What is global state in React?
option_b: State shared across many components often managed with Context or Redux
correct_answer: State shared across many components often managed with Context or Redux
explanation: Global state is accessible throughout the app. Tools like Context Redux Zustand manage global state.

question_text: What is React Context?
option_b: A built-in way to share values across the component tree without prop drilling
correct_answer: A built-in way to share values across the component tree without prop drilling
explanation: Context provides a mechanism to share data like theme or auth between components without passing props manually.

question_text: How do you create a React Context?
explanation: React.createContext(defaultValue) creates a Context object with a Provider and Consumer.

question_text: What is the Context Provider used for?
option_b: Wrapping a component tree to supply a context value to descendants
correct_answer: Wrapping a component tree to supply a context value to descendants
explanation: Context.Provider value={data} makes the data available to all descendant components that call useContext.

question_text: What is lifting state up in React?
option_b: Moving shared state to the nearest common ancestor component
correct_answer: Moving shared state to the nearest common ancestor component
explanation: When siblings need the same data move the state up to their closest common parent and pass it down as props.

question_text: What is Redux?
option_b: A predictable state container for managing global app state
correct_answer: A predictable state container for managing global app state
explanation: Redux stores the entire app state in one place and uses actions and reducers to update it predictably.

question_text: What is a Redux store?
option_b: The single object holding the entire application state tree
correct_answer: The single object holding the entire application state tree
explanation: The Redux store is the single source of truth for the app. Create it with configureStore.

question_text: What is a Redux action?
option_b: A plain JS object with a type field describing what happened in the app
correct_answer: A plain JS object with a type field describing what happened in the app
explanation: Actions describe events: { type: ADD_TODO, payload: Buy milk }. They are dispatched to update state.

question_text: What is a Redux reducer?
option_b: A pure function taking current state and action and returning new state
correct_answer: A pure function taking current state and action and returning new state
explanation: Reducers handle state transitions. They never mutate state directly but return a new state object.

question_text: What is Redux Toolkit?
option_b: The official recommended toolset that simplifies Redux development
correct_answer: The official recommended toolset that simplifies Redux development
explanation: Redux Toolkit provides createSlice configureStore and createAsyncThunk eliminating Redux boilerplate.

question_text: What is createSlice in Redux Toolkit?
option_b: A function that auto-generates action creators and reducer from one definition
correct_answer: A function that auto-generates action creators and reducer from one definition
explanation: createSlice takes name initialState and reducers and returns actions and the reducer automatically.

question_text: What does useSelector do?
option_b: Reads and subscribes to a slice of data from the Redux store
correct_answer: Reads and subscribes to a slice of data from the Redux store
explanation: useSelector(selector) runs the selector against the store and re-renders the component when the result changes.

question_text: What does useDispatch do?
option_b: Returns the Redux dispatch function to send actions to the store
correct_answer: Returns the Redux dispatch function to send actions to the store
explanation: useDispatch gives components access to store.dispatch so they can trigger state changes via actions.

question_text: What is the Provider component from react-redux?
option_b: A component that makes the Redux store available to all child components
correct_answer: A component that makes the Redux store available to all child components
explanation: Wrap your app in Provider store={store} so any component can access the Redux store via hooks.

question_text: What is Zustand?
option_b: A lightweight hook-based state management library for React
correct_answer: A lightweight hook-based state management library for React
explanation: Zustand uses a simple create function and hooks to manage global state with minimal boilerplate.

question_text: What is server state vs client state in React?
option_b: Server state is remote async data; client state is local UI data like form inputs
option_c: Server state is in Redux only
option_d: Client state is in a database
correct_answer: Server state is remote async data; client state is local UI data like form inputs
explanation: Server state comes from APIs and needs syncing. Client state is purely UI data managed locally.

question_text: What is React Query used for?
option_b: Managing server state including fetching caching and syncing remote data
correct_answer: Managing server state including fetching caching and syncing remote data
explanation: React Query handles all aspects of async server state with caching background updates and stale data handling.

question_text: What is the difference between useContext and Redux?
option_b: useContext is built-in and simple; Redux is a separate library for complex global state
correct_answer: useContext is built-in and simple; Redux is a separate library for complex global state
explanation: Context suits small to medium apps. Redux adds structure devtools and middleware for large complex apps.

question_text: What is the purpose of the Redux DevTools extension?
option_b: Inspecting dispatched actions state changes and time-travel debugging
correct_answer: Inspecting dispatched actions state changes and time-travel debugging
explanation: Redux DevTools lets you see every action the state before and after it and replay actions over time.

question_text: What is the useReducer hook used for in local state management?
option_b: Managing complex local state with a reducer and dispatch similar to Redux
correct_answer: Managing complex local state with a reducer and dispatch similar to Redux
explanation: useReducer is great for state with multiple sub-values or when next state depends on complex logic.

question_text: What does dispatch do in useReducer?
option_b: Sends an action object to the reducer to compute and set new state
correct_answer: Sends an action object to the reducer to compute and set new state
explanation: dispatch(action) triggers the reducer with the current state and action returning the new state.

question_text: What is Jotai?
option_b: An atomic state management library for React using primitive atoms
correct_answer: An atomic state management library for React using primitive atoms
explanation: Jotai breaks state into atoms providing granular subscriptions without the boilerplate of Redux.

question_text: What is the purpose of a selector in Redux?
option_b: A function that extracts and derives data from the store state
correct_answer: A function that extracts and derives data from the store state
explanation: Selectors compute derived data from the Redux store making components independent of state shape.

question_text: What is URL state in React apps?
option_b: State stored in the URL such as query params and path segments
correct_answer: State stored in the URL such as query params and path segments
explanation: URL state like search query or current page is accessible via useLocation and useSearchParams.

question_text: What is the useSearchParams hook in React Router?
option_b: Reads and updates URL query parameters
correct_answer: Reads and updates URL query parameters
explanation: useSearchParams returns [searchParams, setSearchParams] for reading and updating the URL query string.

question_text: What is a protected route in React?
option_b: A route that redirects unauthenticated users to a login page
correct_answer: A route that redirects unauthenticated users to a login page
explanation: Protected routes check auth state and use Navigate to redirect if the user is not authenticated.

question_text: What is the difference between push and replace in navigation?
option_b: Push adds a new history entry; replace overwrites the current entry
option_c: Push is for links replace is for forms
correct_answer: Push adds a new history entry; replace overwrites the current entry
explanation: navigate(/path) pushes a new entry. navigate(/path, {replace: true}) replaces the current entry.

question_text: What is HashRouter in React Router?
option_b: A router using the URL hash for routing compatible with static file servers
correct_answer: A router using the URL hash for routing compatible with static file servers
explanation: HashRouter uses #/path routing which works without server configuration but is less clean than BrowserRouter.

question_text: What is route-based code splitting?
option_b: Loading route components lazily to reduce the initial bundle size
correct_answer: Loading route components lazily to reduce the initial bundle size
explanation: Wrapping route components in React.lazy() and Suspense loads them only when their route is visited.

question_text: How do you prevent a link or form from performing its default action in React?

question_text: What is the key idea behind React 18's concurrent rendering?
option_b: Prioritize & interrupt rendering work for better UX
explanation: Concurrent mode allows React to pause low-priority work for urgent updates.

question_text: What naming convention must all custom React hooks follow?
explanation: Starting with “use” is required by Rules of Hooks.

question_text: What are the two fundamental rules you must follow when using hooks?
option_a: Only call at top level & only inside React functions

question_text: Which ESLint plugin catches most common hook mistakes automatically?

question_text: What is the main purpose of the useId() hook introduced in React 18?
option_a: Generate unique & stable IDs for accessibility & forms
explanation: It ensures consistent IDs on server & client (no hydration mismatch).

question_text: Why is useId() better than using Math.random() for form IDs in SSR apps?
option_a: It is stable across server/client renders
explanation: Prevents hydration errors when HTML is rendered on server.

question_text: Which practices improve form accessibility in React?
option_a: Matching id & htmlFor on label & input
